title: Process Management with PM2
description: Keep your app running forever and manage multiple processes with PM2.

slides.title: Why a Process Manager? 🔄
slides.content:
# Keeping Apps Alive

If your Node app crashes, it stops serving requests. A process manager restarts it automatically, manages logs, and can run multiple instances (clustering).

Popular process managers:
- **PM2** (most common)
- Forever
- systemd (Linux built-in)

We'll focus on PM2.

slides.title: Installing and Using PM2 📦
slides.content:
## PM2 Basics

Install globally:
```bash
npm install -g pm2
```

Start your app:
```bash
pm2 start app.js --name my-app
```

Other commands:
- `pm2 list` – list all processes
- `pm2 logs` – show logs
- `pm2 restart my-app` – restart
- `pm2 stop my-app` – stop
- `pm2 delete my-app` – remove from PM2
- `pm2 save` – save current process list to be restored on reboot
- `pm2 startup` – generate startup script to auto-start PM2 on system boot

slides.title: Clustering with PM2 🌐
slides.content:
## Multi-Core Utilization

Node runs in a single thread. To use all CPU cores, you can run multiple instances (clustering). PM2 makes this easy:

```bash
pm2 start app.js -i max  # -i max starts one instance per CPU core
```

PM2 will load-balance requests across instances. Great for performance.

slides.title: Ecosystem File (ecosystem.config.js) 📄

quizData.question: What PM2 flag starts one instance per CPU core?
quizData.explanation: `-i max` tells PM2 to run as many instances as CPU cores.
